skill.name: supabase
skill.description: Navigate a project-specific Supabase schema, table relationships, and query patterns for Empathy Ledger database work.
skill.summary: Understand Supabase tables, relationships, tenant isolation, and common query patterns.
skill.author: Acurioustractor

security_audit.summary: Most static alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, diagrams, table names, and relative documentation links. The meaningful risk is that the skill includes project-specific Supabase MCP endpoints, including a write-capable endpoint with SQL execution tooling, so publication should include a warning.

medium_findings.title: Project-Specific Supabase MCP Access Details
medium_findings.description: The skill documents direct Supabase MCP endpoints for a specific project, including a write-capable configuration and an execute_sql tool. This does not prove malicious intent, but it could enable unintended database changes if used with valid credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The project-specific MCP URLs and available write-oriented tools are explicitly documented. I did not find evidence of automatic credential theft or hidden execution, so confidence is high for elevated operational risk but not for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Project Database Topology Disclosure
medium_findings.description: The skill exposes detailed internal table inventory, relationship maps, and migration names for an application database. This appears intended for developer help, but the detail increases information exposure in a public marketplace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly contains project-specific schema and migration details. This is a contextual exposure risk, although it may be acceptable for an internal skill.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown fences, inline identifiers, TypeScript template strings, and Supabase query examples. They are not executable skill scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside Markdown documentation and code examples. No runnable script file, automatic command invocation, or command injection path was found.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misidentified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts are relative Markdown links to documentation files. They do not read files, write files, or construct filesystem paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both instances are static documentation links. I found no code path that uses these strings for filesystem access.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Schema References
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance findings are table names, relationship diagrams, and database metadata examples. They are not commands that inspect the host environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines describe database foreign keys and tenant relationships. They do not call host reconnaissance commands or request local system information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Write-Capable External Database Tooling Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill points users to a write-capable Supabase MCP endpoint and lists execute_sql as an available tool. This should be restricted to trusted users and reviewed before marketplace publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write-capable endpoint and SQL execution capability are explicit. Risk depends on credentials and deployment context, so this is a dangerous operational pattern rather than confirmed abuse.

content.value_statement: Complex Supabase projects make it hard to find the right table, relationship, or tenant filter. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured database context for safer query planning.
actual_capabilities: Maps major Supabase tables and their relationships for an Empathy Ledger database.
Shows common Supabase query patterns for stories, transcripts, organisations, and tokens.
Identifies tenant isolation patterns that should be included in application queries.
Points to TypeScript type files by database domain.
Lists available Supabase MCP tools and migration locations.
limitations: The schema context is project-specific and may not fit other Supabase projects.
Several table counts and drift notes can become outdated as migrations change.
The skill does not validate permissions before suggesting database access patterns.
Write-capable MCP access requires careful operator review before use.

use_cases.title: Find the right table for a feature
use_cases.description: Use the schema inventory to locate tables, relationships, and type files before implementing a Supabase feature.

use_cases.title: Review tenant isolation in queries
use_cases.description: Check that suggested story, profile, and organisation queries include tenant filters and respect row-level security expectations.

use_cases.title: Understand content distribution data flow
use_cases.description: Trace stories, share tokens, embed tokens, consent records, and audit logs across the documented relationship map.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Table
prompt_templates.prompt: Which Supabase table should I use for [feature or data need]? Explain the related tables and key filters.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Supabase query for [workflow]. Include tenant isolation, related tables, and permission concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a Data Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace how [entity or workflow] moves across the database. Include relationships, audit tables, and likely type files.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Database Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed Supabase change against the documented schema. Flag relationship gaps, tenant risks, and migration impacts.

output_examples.input: Which tables support story sharing and embeds?
output_examples.output: The answer identifies story distributions, story access tokens, embed tokens, consent records, and audit logs. It explains how each table supports sharing.

output_examples.input: How should I query published stories for one tenant?
output_examples.output: The answer recommends first resolving the user tenant, then filtering stories by tenant and status. It notes row-level security expectations.

output_examples.input: Where are organisation and tenant types documented?
output_examples.output: The answer points to the organisation and tenant type file and summarizes the related profile, membership, and tenant tables.

best_practices: Verify current migrations and generated types before relying on documented table counts.
Include tenant filters in every user-facing Supabase query unless a reviewed admin path requires otherwise.
Use read-only MCP access by default and require explicit review before write access.
anti_patterns: Do not use the service role client in user-facing code paths.
Do not copy project-specific MCP endpoints into unrelated projects.
Do not query stories, profiles, or media without tenant isolation.

faq.question: Is this skill general-purpose for any Supabase project?
faq.answer: No. It is tailored to one Empathy Ledger Supabase schema and related type files.

faq.question: Can it write to the database by itself?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation, but it references write-capable MCP access that operators must control.

faq.question: Does it include Supabase credentials?
faq.answer: No credentials were found. It does include project-specific MCP URLs and a project reference.

faq.question: Why is tenant isolation emphasized?
faq.answer: The documented schema is multi-tenant, so queries should filter by tenant to avoid cross-tenant data exposure.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this for query planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides table relationships, query examples, and type file locations for implementation planning.

faq.question: What should be checked before publishing?
faq.answer: Review whether project-specific schema details and write-capable MCP endpoints are appropriate for public marketplace distribution.
